entries.name: AJAX
entries.summary: Minimal Lua Server Pages AJAX example where one LSP page serves HTML, accepts browser POST requests from fetch, and returns JSON without a full page reload.
use_when: The user wants a minimal AJAX example using LSP and browser fetch.
The user wants one page that handles both HTML rendering and POST-based JSON responses.
The user wants to understand request:data and response:json in a small example.
avoid_when: The user wants persistent WebSocket communication.
The user wants SMQ publish/subscribe or RPC over SMQ.
The user wants a larger app structure with separate routes and assets.

entries.name: AJAX Over WebSockets
entries.summary: AJAX/RPC-style browser-to-Lua calls over a persistent WebSocket connection. The example includes a server-side LSP WebSocket endpoint and both modern Promise-based and older jQuery browser clients.
use_when: The user wants request-response browser calls over a persistent WebSocket transport.
The user wants a small custom RPC dispatcher implemented in LSP and Lua.
The user wants to compare modern Promise-based browser code with an older jQuery implementation.
avoid_when: The user wants plain HTTP AJAX without WebSockets.
The user wants SMQ-based RPC or publish/subscribe messaging.
The user wants a production authorization model for exposed server-side functions.

entries.name: anti-session
entries.summary: Demonstrates a stateless multi-page LSP form workflow where encrypted JSON state is carried by the browser instead of stored in the server-side session object.
use_when: The user wants a small multi-step form without allocating server-side session state.
The user wants to learn ba.aesencode, ba.aesdecode, and JSON state handling in LSP pages.
avoid_when: The workflow state is large, long-lived, or needs server-side revocation.
The user needs authentication, authorization, or a replacement for persistent storage.
notes: The AES key is generated at app startup, so browser-carried state from before a restart cannot be decoded.

entries.name: Lua Authentication Examples
entries.summary: Container of three BAS authentication examples showing full-root protection, protected-subdirectory authentication, and manual per-page authentication with shared page helpers. The examples are run with Mako Server commands, but the BAS authentication patterns can be adapted to Xedge app paths.

variants.root: Protects the whole application resource reader with dir:setauth.
variants.subdir: Protects only my-protected/ using a prologue directory.
variants.semiautomatic: Manually authenticates pages through shared emitHeader and emitFooter functions.

use_when: The user wants examples of BAS authenticator setup in Lua.
The user wants to protect an entire app root, a specific subdirectory, or manually protected pages.
The user wants to compare Basic, Digest, and form authentication behavior in Mako Server.
avoid_when: The user wants production credential storage or password hashing rather than demo credentials.
The user wants OAuth, OpenID Connect, WebAuthn, or external identity provider integration.
The user wants an Xedge deployment without reviewing root/subdirectory mounting and app-path adjustments.

entries.name: AWS MQTT
entries.summary: AWS IoT Core MQTT example using ALPN and mutual TLS. The startup script loads AWS certificates, creates a SharkSSL-backed MQTT client, subscribes to topics, and publishes JSON messages.
use_when: The user wants to connect BAS Lua code to AWS IoT Core over MQTT.
The user needs an ALPN and mutual TLS MQTT example.
The user wants MQTT subscription, publishing, and cleanup lifecycle code.
avoid_when: The user does not have AWS IoT Core certificates and broker endpoint settings.
The user wants a generic local MQTT broker example.
The user wants production secret storage without adapting certificate handling.

entries.name: CGI
entries.summary: Lua CGI adapter for Mako Server-style deployments using the forkpty plugin and external CGI scripts. It mounts a CGI directory into the BAS virtual file system and translates requests into CGI process execution.
use_when: The user wants to study or modify a CGI compatibility layer for BAS/Mako.
The user needs to run external shell or Python CGI scripts behind a BAS directory.
The user is working on Linux, macOS, or QNX-like environments with forkpty support.
avoid_when: The user wants generic Xedge deployment.
The user wants an embedded target without external process execution.
The user wants a pure LSP/Lua server-side implementation without CGI scripts.

entries.name: Command Environment
entries.summary: Container with two small LSP examples demonstrating the BAS request/response command environment through response:include and response:forward behavior.

variants.forward: Page control transfer and command environment behavior using response:forward.

use_when: The user wants to understand BAS request/response command environment lifetime.
The user wants examples of response:include or response:forward.
The user needs to compare per-request variables with session state.
avoid_when: The user wants a single cohesive application.
The user wants authentication, database, or real-time messaging examples.
The user wants browser-only JavaScript examples.

entries.name: CryptoIO
entries.summary: Reusable Lua module that wraps a BAS IO with AES-GCM encryption, plus a startup example that verifies encrypted file round-tripping and mounts an encrypted Web File Server/WebDAV endpoint.
use_when: The user wants encrypted-at-rest file storage using BAS IO APIs.
The user wants to wrap existing IO objects with transparent encryption.
The user wants an encrypted Web File Server or WebDAV example.
avoid_when: The user wants database encryption instead of file IO encryption.
The user wants to change encrypted file format without migration planning.
The user wants production key policy without reviewing the target trust model.

entries.name: Documentation Examples
entries.summary: Index directory for runnable examples referenced from Real Time Logic documentation. The current child example is a shopping-cart route demonstrating BAS virtual file system directory logic.

variants.cart: Shopping-cart route example using a BAS directory function and response forwarding.

use_when: The user wants examples referenced from BAS documentation.
The user wants the shopping-cart VFS directory example.
The user is adding or organizing documentation companion examples.
avoid_when: The user wants one runnable app at the docex root.
The user wants a full e-commerce cart implementation.
The user wants examples not tied to BAS documentation.

entries.name: Dynamic Navigation Menu
entries.summary: Multi-page LSP application with shared dynamic navigation, custom 404 handling, and form authentication using a JSON user database with HA1 password hashes.
use_when: The user wants a protected multi-page LSP app with shared navigation.
The user wants form authentication with hashed credentials.
The user wants custom 404 forwarding and public login assets.
avoid_when: The user wants a client-side SPA framework.
The user wants OAuth, SSO, or WebAuthn authentication.
The user wants a database-backed user management system.

entries.name: Email And Logging
entries.summary: Mixed collection of SMTP examples and Mako logging/email examples. Direct text, HTML, and EML email examples use socket.mail and can be adapted to Xedge email settings; the log variant is Mako-specific.

variants.eml: EML parser and template email example.
variants.log: Mako-specific mako.log and emailed error-reporting example.

use_when: The user wants direct SMTP email examples using socket.mail.
The user wants HTML or EML-based email templates.
The user wants to understand Mako Server email logging behavior.
avoid_when: The user wants a single homogeneous app root.
The user wants Xedge deployment of the mako.log variant without adaptation.
The user wants production secret handling without replacing demo SMTP configuration.

entries.name: ESP32 Xedge32 Examples
entries.summary: Collection of independent Xedge32 examples for ESP32-class hardware. The examples demonstrate LSP and XLua for GPIO, I2C sensors, camera capture and streaming, Wi-Fi diagnostics, ADC audio streaming, PWM servo control, MQTT, SMQ, and Mako Server simulation support.

variants.misc: Independent Xedge32 scripts and pages; upload only the files needed for the selected hardware example.
variants.simulator: Support files for running selected Xedge32 examples in Mako Server simulation mode.
variants.grafana: Grafana dashboard JSON for the ESP32/MySQL weather-station tutorial.

use_when: The user wants small Xedge32 examples for ESP32 hardware features.
The user wants to adapt GPIO, I2C, ADC, PWM, camera, Wi-Fi, MQTT, or SMQ code for an ESP32 board.
The user wants independent LSP or XLua files that can be uploaded selectively to an Xedge32 app.
The user wants to run selected Xedge32 examples in Mako Server simulation mode before hardware deployment.
The user wants the ESP32/MySQL weather-station Grafana dashboard asset.
avoid_when: The user wants a normal Mako Server example that runs directly with one local app command.
The user wants a single cohesive web application where every file in the directory is deployed together.
The user wants generic browser-only examples without ESP32 or Xedge32 APIs.
The user wants production-ready board support without confirming GPIO pins, I2C pins, ADC channels, PWM settings, camera pin maps, power wiring, and MQTT settings.

entries.name: WebDAV And Web File Server
entries.summary: BAS Web File Server and WebDAV example mounted at /fs/ with simple authentication, writable IO selection, lock-directory setup, and cleanup on unload.
use_when: The user wants to mount a BAS Web File Server.
The user wants a WebDAV endpoint example.
The user wants to adapt file storage and authentication for Mako or Xedge.
avoid_when: The user wants encrypted-at-rest storage; use CryptoIO instead.
The user wants SSO-protected file server behavior; use fs-sso instead.
The user wants a database-backed document system.

entries.name: File Server SSO
entries.summary: Microsoft Entra ID OpenID Connect SSO example that protects a BAS Web File Server/WebDAV endpoint and provides session URLs for non-browser WebDAV clients.
use_when: The user wants Microsoft Entra ID SSO for a BAS application.
The user wants to protect a Web File Server with SSO.
The user needs session URL behavior for WebDAV clients that cannot perform browser login.
avoid_when: The user wants simple username/password authentication.
The user wants WebAuthn, RADIUS, or local JSON-user authentication.
The user wants Xedge deployment without replacing mako.conf secret storage and validating HTTPS/origin settings.

entries.name: GitHub IO
entries.summary: LuaIO-compatible GitHub repository driver that exposes GitHub contents as BAS file storage and can be mounted behind the Web File Server/WebDAV endpoint.
use_when: The user wants to expose a GitHub repository as a BAS IO.
The user wants a WebDAV/Web File Server backed by GitHub.
The user wants to adapt repository-backed storage for Xedge IDE integration.
avoid_when: The user wants high-volume binary file storage.
The user cannot provide a GitHub repository and suitable token.
The user wants local filesystem storage without remote API rate limits.

entries.name: HTML Form
entries.summary: Beginner LSP example showing an HTML form, POST handling with request:data, and simple session storage in one page.
use_when: The user wants a minimal form-handling example.
The user wants to understand POST data in LSP.
The user wants a tiny session example.
avoid_when: The user wants production authentication.
The user wants AJAX or WebSocket form handling.
The user wants a multi-page application structure.

entries.name: htmx Examples
entries.summary: Introductory htmx plus LSP example where the browser requests a server-rendered HTML fragment and swaps it into the page.

variants.introduction: Basic htmx example using hx-get to load an LSP-generated user list fragment.

use_when: The user wants a small htmx and LSP example.
The user wants server-rendered HTML fragments instead of JSON.
The user wants a lightweight alternative to SPA frameworks.
avoid_when: The user wants a complete dashboard; use Light-Dashboard.
The user wants WebSocket or SMQ updates.
The user wants a JSON REST API example.

entries.name: JSON File Server
entries.summary: Authentication and authorization example using a BAS JSON user database, ACL authorizer constraints, Digest authentication, request:login, and a protected Web File Server/WebDAV endpoint.
use_when: The user wants JSON-backed BAS authentication and authorization.
The user wants file-server access control by user, role, path, and method.
The user wants to test request:login with an authorizer.
avoid_when: The user wants OAuth, SSO, WebAuthn, or external identity providers.
The user wants encrypted file storage; use CryptoIO.
The user wants a production credential store without replacing demo users.

entries.name: Light Dashboard
entries.summary: Three database-free embedded dashboard UI variants for Mako Server and Xedge. Designed for small embedded targets such as routers, gateways, RTOS devices, and firmware-backed products.

variants.htmx: HTMX dashboard using Pure.css and fragment navigation.
variants.custom: HTMX dashboard using custom CSS, two-level navigation, CMS-level SMQ, page scopes, and page-scoped SMQ RPC.

use_when: The user wants a ready-to-run embedded dashboard UI.
The user wants a small database-free CMS-style page and menu system.
The user wants an HTMX dashboard that behaves like an SPA without a SPA framework.
The user wants full-page fallback when JavaScript is disabled or direct URLs are loaded.
The user wants persistent SMQ behavior across HTMX page fragments.
The user wants page-scoped SMQ subscriptions, cleanup, or RPC over SMQ.
The user wants an example that can run under Mako Server or be packaged for Xedge.
avoid_when: The user wants a database-driven CMS for larger Embedded Linux or QNX systems.
The user wants a generic REST API example without dashboard UI.
The user wants low-level SMQ protocol examples without the dashboard/CMS layer.

entries.name: Lua Debug
entries.summary: Mako Server Lua/LSP debugging tutorial companion for Visual Studio Code and the BAS debug monitor, with a primary debug target app and helper FileServer app for remote source mapping.
notes: As written this is Mako Server focused. It can be adapted by AI to an Xedge-oriented debugging workflow, but requires changes to debug monitor startup, app loading, restart behavior, and source mapping.
use_when: The user wants to debug Lua or LSP code with VS Code and Mako Server.
The user wants to understand source mapping and debug monitor setup.
The user wants a starting point for adapting Mako debugging concepts to Xedge.
avoid_when: The user wants an app feature example rather than debugger workflow.
The user wants generic Xedge deployment with no Mako debug monitor assumptions.
The user wants browser-only JavaScript debugging.

entries.name: MinnowSim
entries.summary: Server-side simulated device backend for the Minnow SPA tutorial. It requires the separate RealTimeLogic/MinnowServer repository for the browser SPA and must be run as a two-app Mako setup.
use_when: The user wants the backend/device side of the Minnow SPA tutorial.
The user wants a WebSocket JSON application with simulated embedded state.
The user has or is willing to clone the companion MinnowServer repository.
avoid_when: The user expects this directory alone to serve the SPA UI.
The companion MinnowServer repository is unavailable and cannot be cloned or otherwise provided.
The user wants real ESP32 GPIO without adapting the simulator branch.
The user wants an SMQ example rather than raw WebSocket JSON messaging.
prerequisites: The companion repository https://github.com/RealTimeLogic/MinnowServer must be available as a sibling checkout or at a known path.
If MinnowServer is missing, clone it with: git clone https://github.com/RealTimeLogic/MinnowServer
Run Mako with MinnowServer/www mounted as /minnow/ and MinnowSim/www mounted as the root app.
setup: Recommended layout: place MinnowServer and LSP-Examples in the same parent directory.
From the common parent directory: mako -lminnow::MinnowServer/www -l::LSP-Examples/MinnowSim/www
From inside LSP-Examples with MinnowServer as a sibling: mako -lminnow::../MinnowServer/www -l::MinnowSim/www

entries.name: LMQTT Broker
entries.summary: Lua MQTT 3.1.1/5.0 broker for BAS-derived runtimes, with TLS/plain listeners, authentication and publish-policy callbacks, in-process client support, design notes, and Python tests.
use_when: The user wants to host an MQTT broker in a BAS/Mako/Xedge app.
The user wants a compact Lua MQTT broker implementation.
The user wants broker callbacks for authentication or publish policy.
avoid_when: The user needs retained messages, persistent sessions, QoS 1/2, shared subscriptions, or topic aliases as-is.
The user only needs an MQTT client connecting to an external broker.
The user cannot open listener ports on the target deployment.

entries.name: MySQL And Redis Drivers
entries.summary: OpenResty-style MySQL and Redis drivers adapted for BAS socket and cosocket behavior, with a reusable package layout and LSP test pages for blocking and ba.socket.event execution.
use_when: The user wants MySQL or Redis access from BAS Lua.
The user wants OpenResty-style driver compatibility.
The user wants a persistent MySQL connection pattern for BAS cosockets.
avoid_when: The user does not have reachable MySQL or Redis services.
The user wants ORM-style database abstractions.
The user wants connection pooling without implementing it.

entries.name: OAuth 2.0 Example
entries.summary: GitHub OAuth 2.0 client example converted from PHP to LSP, demonstrating redirect login, state validation, token exchange, session storage, and GitHub API calls.
use_when: The user wants a small GitHub OAuth 2.0 flow in LSP.
The user wants to understand OAuth redirects and callback handling.
The user wants a starting point for adapting OAuth to a more secure BAS app.
avoid_when: The user wants production credential handling as-is.
The user wants Xedge compatibility without replacing mako.sharkclient and embedded secrets.
The user wants OpenID Connect/SSO; use fs-sso instead.

entries.name: PostgreSQL
entries.summary: Demonstrates a persistent PostgreSQL connection helper for a Mako Server application, with database work run on a BAS thread and rendered from an LSP page.
use_when: The user wants to reuse one PostgreSQL connection from a Mako Server app.
The user wants to study deferred responses and database work on BAS threads.
avoid_when: The user needs a generic Xedge example without a native PostgreSQL binding.
The user wants a production-ready database abstraction or connection pool.
notes: Requires a host-compiled pgsql.so and valid database credentials in mako.conf.

entries.name: QNX
entries.summary: Contains QNX-specific BAS integration code, currently a PPS-to-SMQ bridge that exposes QNX Persistent Publish Subscribe messages to browser and device clients through SMQ.
use_when: The user needs to bridge QNX PPS data into an SMQ-based web or device UI.
The user is working on BAS code running in a QNX environment.
avoid_when: The user needs a standalone local Mako demo without QNX PPS.
The user needs generic Xedge compatibility.

variants.PPS: QNX Persistent Publish Subscribe bridge module that maps PPS messages to SMQ topics.

notes: This directory is platform-specific and does not contain a complete standalone web app.

entries.name: RADIUS
entries.summary: Demonstrates RADIUS-backed authentication in a BAS web app by wiring a Lua RADIUS client into BAS authentication callbacks.
use_when: The user wants BAS authentication backed by a RADIUS server.
The user wants an example of UDP protocol code combined with BAS auth callbacks.
avoid_when: The user needs OAuth, SAML, or a browser-only authentication example.
The target cannot reach a RADIUS server.
notes: Xedge use may require deployment and loader adjustments for the target environment.

entries.name: require-test
entries.summary: Shows how a Mako Server app uses mako.createloader(io) so Lua require and dofile can load modules from the app IO tree.
use_when: The user wants to understand app-local Lua module loading in Mako Server.
The user needs examples of require paths under www/.lua and nested module directories.
avoid_when: The user needs generic Xedge module loading guidance.
The user wants an application feature example rather than loader behavior.
notes: This example intentionally targets Mako Server loader behavior.

entries.name: REST
entries.summary: Provides a minimal REST-style Lua router for BAS applications and an in-memory /api/users example service.
use_when: The user wants to build REST-style endpoints using BAS directories and Lua request handling.
The user wants a small router module that can be reused in an embedded web app.
avoid_when: The user needs a full web framework or persistent database-backed service out of the box.
The user needs authentication, authorization, or rate limiting already built in.
notes: The example data store is in memory and resets on app restart.

entries.name: SMQ-examples
entries.summary: Index of Simple Message Queue examples covering direct messaging, RPC over SMQ, IoT chat and LED control, browser switch/bulb demos, multiplayer game variants, and a Mako-specific cluster experiment.
use_when: The user wants examples of SMQ publish/subscribe, one-to-one messaging, or browser/device communication.
The user wants to build an embedded real-time UI using SMQ.
avoid_when: The user needs a single self-contained app with one startup command for the entire directory.
The user needs generic WebSocket examples unrelated to SMQ.

variants.BlobArena: AI-generated multiplayer browser game variants using SMQ.
variants.RPC: Promise-style browser RPC over SMQ with Lua server dispatch.
variants.LightSwitch-And-LightBulb-App: Browser switch and bulb pages communicating through SMQ.

notes: The cluster subexample is Mako-specific as written and should not be treated as generic Xedge code.

entries.name: socket-examples
entries.summary: Collection of BAS socket and WebSocket examples covering UDP, NTP, blocking sockets, cosockets, WebSocket services, a proxy, a simple server, and an Eliza chatbot.
use_when: The user wants to learn BAS socket, UDP, WebSocket, or cosocket APIs.
The user wants small isolated socket examples rather than a full application.
avoid_when: The user needs only SMQ messaging examples.
The target cannot support the required outbound or inbound socket operations.
notes: Blocking examples are educational and should be reviewed before use on constrained embedded targets.

entries.name: Sparkplug
entries.summary: Demonstrates MQTT Sparkplug B with an Edge of Network node example and a Sparkplug Explorer that subscribes to and decodes Sparkplug payloads.
use_when: The user wants to work with MQTT Sparkplug B in BAS, Mako Server, or Xedge.
The user wants examples of Sparkplug birth, data, command, and state flows.
avoid_when: The user needs plain MQTT without Sparkplug topic and payload rules.
The target cannot reach the configured MQTT broker.

variants.EoN: Edge of Network node example that publishes Sparkplug birth and data messages.
variants.SparkplugExplorer: MQTT Sparkplug explorer that subscribes to wildcard topics and decodes payloads.

notes: Broker settings and credentials in the demo files must be reviewed before production use.

entries.name: SQLite
entries.summary: Index of SQLite examples covering a basic Lua SQLite tutorial, URL-to-database wiki mapping, and shared-connection behavior under request load.
use_when: The user wants to learn SQLite usage from BAS Lua and LSP pages.
The user wants examples of URL-to-database mapping or shared SQLite connection behavior.
avoid_when: The user needs an Xedge-ready SQLite example without Mako-specific app startup assumptions.
The user needs a production database layer with migrations and access control.

variants.Shared-Connection: Shared SQLite connection and locking behavior example.

notes: Each SQLite subdirectory is a separate app with its own README and run command.

entries.name: TraceLogger
entries.summary: Mako Server TraceLogger client that connects to another BAS-enabled device and forwards the remote trace stream to the local console or log.
use_when: The user wants to monitor trace output from another BAS-enabled device using a local Mako Server process.
The user wants to study the TraceLogger service protocol, HTTP client upgrade, and socket receive loop.
avoid_when: The user needs a generic Xedge application without Mako configuration and local console/log assumptions.
The user needs a browser-only TraceLogger UI without a Lua trace-forwarding client.
notes: Set trpeer in mako.conf before running; localhost is intentionally rejected.

entries.name: upload
entries.summary: Two BAS file-upload examples: a simpler blocking upload flow and an asynchronous upload flow for higher concurrency, both with form and drag-and-drop browser UI.
use_when: The user wants to add file or firmware upload support to a BAS web application.
The user wants to compare simple blocking upload handling with ba.create.upload-based asynchronous handling.
avoid_when: The target does not provide writable storage for uploaded files.
The user needs a complete file manager rather than focused upload examples.

variants.blocking: Simpler request-driven upload example and recommended starting point.
variants.asynchronous: Advanced ba.create.upload-based example for higher upload concurrency.

notes: Xedge use requires adapting writable storage paths and IO selection to the target.

entries.name: Web-Shell
entries.summary: Linux-oriented browser shell that uses ba.forkpty for a server-side pseudo-terminal and SMQ plus xterm.js for the browser terminal UI.
use_when: The user wants to study ba.forkpty, PTY lifecycle handling, and browser terminal integration.
The user wants a Linux web shell example that bridges terminal input and output over SMQ.
avoid_when: The target does not include ba.forkpty or a Linux-style shell.
The user needs a safe production remote shell without a full authentication and authorization design.
The user needs a generic Xedge-compatible example.
notes: This example is intentionally Linux/Mako-oriented and should not be exposed without strong access control.

entries.name: WebAuthn
entries.summary: BAS WebAuthn/passkey module with two example applications: an introductory registration/authentication flow and a fuller protected-login flow with optional email activation.
use_when: The user wants to add passkey/FIDO2 WebAuthn authentication to a BAS-powered web application.
The user wants examples of server-side WebAuthn callbacks, user database persistence, and protected app login.
avoid_when: The deployment cannot provide trusted HTTPS, a valid origin, or a browser-supported WebAuthn environment.
The user only needs simple password or RADIUS authentication.

variants.example2: More complete protected-login example with registration activation and optional email.

notes: Mako is the documented local run target; Xedge use requires correct .config deployment, trusted HTTPS/origin setup, storage, and email configuration.

entries.name: Xedge App Deployment
entries.summary: Source layout for the MyApp.zip Xedge deployment tutorial, showing .config metadata, .preload startup logic, an LSP page, and an .xlua script packaged from the www directory.
use_when: The user wants to learn how an Xedge application ZIP should be structured.
The user wants a small source example for packaging and uploading an Xedge app.
avoid_when: The user needs a feature-rich web app instead of a deployment packaging example.
The user needs an ESP32-specific hardware example.
notes: Create MyApp.zip from inside www with: zip -D -q -u -r -9 ../MyApp.zip .
